content.full_text:
Fern Polrrrcel PRACTICEs CouulsSIoN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
March 10,2006
J. Antonio Barbosa
Agricultural Labor Relations Board
915 Capitol Mall, 3'd Floor
Sacramento, CA95814
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-06-027
Dear Mr. Barbosa:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of yourself and
employees of the Agricultural Labor Relations Board regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").l The Commission's advice is limited to matters arising
under the Act. (Regulation 18329(b)(8XD), copy enclosed.) We urge you to check with
the state Attorney General's office to determine if there are other laws that maybe
applicable in light of the facts you present.
QUESTION
Does the acceptance by a designated employee of the Agricultural Labor
Relations Board of a discount on wireless telephone services and equipment offered by
Verizon Wireless constitute a gift when it is also offered to all state employees?
CONCLUSION
No. Provided the discount is made in the regular course of business conducted by
Verizon Wireless, the discount will not be considered a gift to designated employees for
purposes of the Act's reporting, gift limit, or conflict-of-interest rules.
FACTS
The Agricultural Labor Relations Board has received a general invitation from
Verizon Wireless which informs the agency of discounts provided to state employees
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-06-027
Page No. 2
should they sign up for afamily calling plan and/or wish to purchase cell phone
equipment at discounted prices.
You are a designated employee for the AgriculturalLabor Relations Board and
want to find out if there is some prohibition in the Act that requires designated employees
not to accept this type of general offer from a third party although the offer was extended
to all state employees on the same basis.
ANALYSIS
The Gift Disclosure Provisions of the Act
Public officials, other than those covered by section 87200, who make, participate
in making, or use their official position to influence governmental decisions, must
disclose certain economic interests as required by their respective agency's conflict of
interest code. (Sections 87300 and 87302.) Referred to as designated employees, they
report the receipt of gifts valued at $50.00 or more in the aggregate in a calendar year
from a single source if the source is a type required to be disclosed under the conflict-of-
interest code for the agency. (Sections 82030 and 87302.)
Gift Limitations
Designated employees may not accept gifts from any single source totaling more
than $360 in a calendar year if such employees are required to report receiving income or
gifts from that source on their statements of economic interests. (Section 89503(c).)
Discounts as Gifts
Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as
o'Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless
the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to
members of the public wtthout regard to fficial status." (Emphasis
added.)
Thus, a discount is a gift unless the discount is made in the regular course of
business, to members of the public, without regard to official status.
In its opinionin In re Russel, (1975) I FPPC Ops. 191, the Commission concluded
that the Act does not impose any restrictions or reporting requirements on those who
offer discounts to all state employees or on public officials who take advantage of such
discounts, if the discount is uniformly offered to all state employees. Under such


File No. A-06-027
Page No. 3
circumstances, the discount is not considered to be a gift. (schectman Advice Letter, No
A-96-218-) The Commission reasoned that adiscount thai is available to all employees
of the State, without tegard to what office or position they hold in state goveflrment, is a
discount made available "without regard to oificial status-.,,
Therefore, we conclude that designated employees of the Agricultural Labor
Relations Board who choose to take advantageoith" discount offered by Verizon
Wireless will not be receivin g a gift2 that is either reportable on their Statements of
Economic Interests or subject to the gift rimits of seclion g9503 of the Act.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
VJ:rd
I:\Adviceltrs\06-027
2 Because the discount will not be considered a gift under section 82028,it will also not subject an
employee to the conflict-of-interest provisions of sectionJg7l00 and g7103.

Does the acceptance by a designated employee of the Agricultural Labor

Verizon Wireless constitute a gift when it is also offered to all state employees?

purposes of the Act's reporting, gift limit, or conflict-of-interest rules.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the acceptance by a designated employee of the Agricultural Labor
Relations Board of a discount on wireless telephone services and equipment offered by
Verizon Wireless constitute a gift when it is also offered to all state employees?

CONCLUSION: No. Provided the discount is made in the regular course of business conducted by
Verizon Wireless, the discount will not be considered a gift to designated employees for
purposes of the Act's reporting, gift limit, or conflict-of-interest rules.
